personality_traits:
Character biography: Character biography
The first book explains Noddy's origins. He is made by a woodcarver in a toy shop but runs away after the man begins to make a wooden lion, which scares Noddy. As he wanders through the woods naked, penniless, and homeless, he meets Big Ears, a friendly brownie. Big Ears decides that Noddy is a toy and takes him to live in Toyland. He generously provides Noddy with a set of clothing and a house. While Noddy is quite happy to be a toy, the citizens of Toyland are not sure that
raw_description:
Created by Enid Blyton, Noddy first appeared in the Sunday Graphic on 5 June 1949, the same year as Blyton's first daily Noddy strip for the London Evening Standard.[1] In November that year Noddy Goes to Toyland, the first of at least two dozen books in the series, was published. The idea was conceived by one of Blyton's publishers, Sampson, Low, Marston and Company, who in 1949 arranged a meeting between Blyton and the Dutch illustrator Harmsen van der Beek. Despite having to communicate via an interpreter, he provided some initial sketches of how Toyland and its characters would be represented. Four days after the meeting, Blyton sent the text of the first two Noddy books to her publisher, to be forwarded to van der Beek.[4]
The Noddy books became one of Blyton's most successful and best-known series, and were hugely popular in the 1950s.[5] An extensive range of sub-series, spin-offs and strip books was produced throughout the decade, including Noddy's Library, Noddy's Garage of Books, Noddy's Castle of Books, Noddy's Toy Station of Books and Noddy's Shop of Books.[6]
The first television adaptation of Noddy called The Adventures of Noddy was first broadcast in 1955, which was narrated by Enid Blyton.[1][7][8][9] The programme would also broadcast in German on ARD in 1963.[10]
Kellogg's would acquire the rights to use Noddy on advertisements in 1955.[1]
Noddy continued to be successful in the 1960s; by 1962, 26 million copies of Noddy had been sold.[1][a] In 1963, Noddy was featured in the animated short film Noddy Goes to Toyland, it was produced by Arthur Humberstone for Enid Blyton.[11] Blyton concluded several of her long-running series in 1963. Blyton published her last book in the Noddy series, Noddy and the Aeroplane, in February 1964.[1]
By the early 1960s, some 146 different companies were involved in merchandising Noddy alone.[12] Bestime released the Little Noddy Car Game in 1953 and the Little Noddy Leap Frog Game in 1955, and in 1956 American manufacturer Parker Brothers released Little Noddy's Taxi Game, a board game which features Noddy driving about town, picking up various characters.[13] Bestime released its Plywood Noddy Jigsaws series in 1957 and a Noddy jigsaw series featuring cards appeared from 1963, with illustrations by Robert Lee. Arrow Games became the chief producer of Noddy jigsaws in the late 1970s and early 1980s.[12]
In 1975, another television adaptation simply titled Noddy was broadcast. It was produced and directed by Brian Cosgrove and Mark Hall under their company Stop Frame Productions and was narrated by Richard Briers. It broadcast on ITV from 1975 to 1976.[9][14][15]
In 1992, a Noddy television series produced by BBC Worldwide and Cosgrove Hall called Noddy's Toyland Adventures was broadcast on the Children's BBC.
In January 1996, Trocadero PLC (later known as "Chorion Limited") acquired Darrell Waters Ltd., the holding company for the estate of children's author Enid Blyton for £14.6 million. They soon formed a new subsidiary - Enid Blyton Ltd. to handle all intellectual properties, character brands, and media in Blyton's works, including Noddy.[16]
In 1998, producer Rick Siggelkow brought Noddy into the North American market in a television series called The Noddy Shop, but with all of the live-action scenes filmed in Canada. Much like how Siggelkow introduced Thomas & Friends to American audiences through Shining Time Station, the series incorporated footage from Noddy's Toyland Adventures re-dubbed with American accents.[1] In its first season on PBS, the show was seen by an average of 2.5 million viewers per episode, higher than Sesame Street's average during the same year.[17] Actor Jack Nicholson and Friends star Lisa Kudrow had their picture taken with Noddy at the Aids Paediatric event in New York, USA in June 1999.[18]
By 1999, 200 million Noddy books had been sold in 27 languages. Noddy is known as "Oui Oui" in France, "Doddi" in Iceland, "Purzelknirps" in Germany and "Hilitos" in Spain.[9][18]
A new Noddy television series called Make Way for Noddy was first announced to be in production in October 2000, with 100 11-minute episodes and a feature-length Christmas special announced to be in production for a 2001 delivery.[19] In January 2001, SD Entertainment were announced to be producers on the series and they announced it would be their first project.[20] In May 2002, UK's Channel Five announced that it had commissioned 100 episodes of the series,[21] airing in September of that year.[22] The range of newly developed TV shows at Chorion began to expand internationally, with Noddy becoming the most recognised children's character in France in 2003 and sold to the Chinese market in 2004,[23] and airing in the US on PBS Kids in 2005.[24]
In 2005, a set of 100 new, two-minute TV interstitials were created by Chorion. These interstitials, entitled Say it with Noddy, feature Noddy learning words in a variety of foreign languages. They also introduced Noddy
